query: Latest news June-July 2026 on Johor state election campaign, expected voter turnout projections, analyst forecasts, and any factors affecting turnout like Singapore commuter voters. What turnout numbers are analysts and Election Commission SPR predicting for July 11 2026?
answer:
I've completed the research. Here's a summary of the key findings on the 2026 Johor state election turnout question:

## Bottom Line

**There is no official SPR/Election Commission turnout forecast** — the EC has not published a predicted turnout number. Turnout is instead the central analytical debate, benchmarked against 2022's historic low.

### Key numbers
- **2022 baseline:** 54.92% turnout (historic modern low; The Star tracker cites 53.55%) — driven by COVID border closures and political fatigue. Only 1.43M of ~2.6M cast ballots. By contrast, 2018 Johor turnout was 84.51%.
- **2026 electorate:** 2,727,926 registered voters; polling July 11 (Saturday), early voting July 7; all 56 seats contested (BN vs PH main battle).

### The clearest analyst forecast (Mazlan Ali, UTM, via CNA)
> "If the voter turnout is below 60 per cent, BN will likely win but if the voter turnout exceeds 75 or reaches 80 per cent, PH could control the state of Johor."

Analysts broadly call turnout **the decisive factor**, with PH's challenge being to mobilise urban supporters. Muhyiddin (PN) warned low Malay turnout could decide the result.

### Singapore commuter / out-of-state voters — the pivotal swing factor
- ~400,000 JB residents cross the Causeway into Singapore. In 2022, closed borders kept these (mostly PH-leaning urban) voters home. Borders are now open, so their return is the key variable.
- PH arranged **extra KL–Johor trains** and **expedited Singapore customs/immigration lanes** to boost return turnout.
- In 2022, 8 of 10 lowest-turnout seats were PH/MUDA-held (e.g., Perling 42.2%, Skudai 43.8%).

Expectation: turnout should **recover above 2022's ~55%** (no pandemic, open borders), but the exact level is the unknown that determines the outcome. Most seat projections still favor BN retention, implying analysts expect moderate rather than GE15-style 70%+ turnout.

sources.title: The ghost of 2022: Will voter apathy again haunt Johor's July 11 election?
sources.snippet: When Johor last voted in March 2022, it witnessed a dismal turnout of just 54.92 per cent. Out of nearly 2.6 million eligible voters, only 1,426,573 ballots were cast. In 2018, turnout stood at 84.51 per cent. With 2,725,559 registered voters now on the rolls.

sources.title: Johor state election set for Jul 11, Negeri Sembilan to go to polls on Aug 1
sources.snippet: Analyst Mazlan Ali (UTM): 'If the voter turnout is below 60 per cent, BN will likely win but if the voter turnout exceeds 75 or reaches 80 per cent, PH could control the state of Johor.' Johor recorded ~54% turnout in 2022 with foreign voters including those in Singapore likely not returning due to border closures.

sources.title: Johor Polls: Muhyiddin warns low Malay voter turnout could determine election outcome
sources.snippet: Muhyiddin: 'Sometimes only about 55% turn up to vote. Where are the other 45%?' Low turnout among Malays could determine the outcome.

sources.snippet: A total of 2,727,926 registered voters are eligible, comprising 2,703,175 ordinary voters, 12,041 military and 12,710 police and spouses. Early voting July 7, polling July 11.

sources.title: 16th Johor State Election Officially Begins Today
sources.snippet: 2,727,926 registered voters; 56 seats; nomination process at 56 centres; early voting July 7, 2026; polling July 11, 2026.

sources.snippet: EC issued 24,677 postal ballot papers across all 56 constituencies; 23,288 under Form 1A.

sources.snippet: PH voters need to be motivated to turn out; PH ministers arranged additional KL-Johor trains and expedited customs/immigration lanes for voters crossing from Singapore.

sources.snippet: 3.6 million voters across both states; a third of the electorate under 30 with nearly half undecided; turnout and mobilisation key.

sources.snippet: Young and first-time voters (Undi18/automatic registration) expected to be decisive kingmakers in marginal seats.
